opp.ais_why:
• Sole Avoma meeting is a not_recorded DPW (Digital Procurement World) event stand visit on 8 Oct 2025 with Cedric Merlet from Ipsen Pharma. No transcript-level AI evidence is retrievable from this unrecorded meeting. ANA and Merlin are in product scope alongside a broad S2P (eProcurement, iContract, iRequest, iRisk, iSource) ‚Äî suggesting AI is in Zycus's positioning consideration.
• Qualified stage at $200K with BD_Engagement Engaged status, but LastActivityDate is null indicating limited recent traction beyond the DPW touchpoint. DPW attendance suggests Ipsen's interest in modern procurement (industry showcase event) but the specific Ipsen-Zycus engagement evidence is thin. The 6+ month gap since the only captured meeting is a yellow flag.
• Medium confidence: Engaged BD + ANA/Merlin in product scope are positives but only 1 unrecorded meeting prevents confirming AI-first demand. Defaulted DOWN at 5/6 boundary. Would move UP with a verified recorded meeting that captures Cedric Merlet or another Ipsen stakeholder asking about Merlin/ANA capability; would drop with continued dormancy.

sources: NextStep (historical): reference to 'make-or-buy discussion from the FOP event'
Salesforce Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, Value_to_Customer__c fields (if present): to be read in Q3 query
metrics.narrative: No quantified business case, ROI, or savings target has been articulated by the relevant stakeholder notes reference a 'make-or-buy discussion from the FOP event' (a prospect event), implying Ipsen is evaluating build-vs-buy for procurement transformation, but no specific pain metric (cost per PO, procurement cycle time, supplier spend under management, manual effort in contracting) has been captured. Without a quantified value case, the deal lacks commercial urgency and the $200k amount is likely a rough placeholder rather than a validated budget. The move is to use the formal discovery call (planned but not yet confirmed scheduled) to extract the specific operational pain and financial impact Ipsen is solving for, and to anchor the Merlin AI story to a measurable efficiency gain (e.g. contract cycle-time reduction, negotiation cost savings).

sources: Contact roles: Guillaume Quantin (Evaluator), last contact 2026-06-15 per LastActivityDate
NextStep 25 June 2026: 'No feedback from Guillaume'
NextStep 15 June 2026: 'Ask for project confirmation propose a demonstration meeting on site' (implies Guillaume was asked but has not replied)
champion.narrative: Guillaume Quantin (Global Strategic Sourcing Director, Evaluator role) is the only engaged contact and is the developing champion candidate, but he has gone silent since 15 June 2026. The NextStep notes he has not provided feedback on the project confirmation or demo request (15 June 2026 ask), and the planned RFP (July 2026) has not materialized. Pierre (the deal owner) is planning a chase-call after 6 July 2026. Guillaume's access to the Decision Makers (Cedric Merlet VP, Said Tamazirt Head of CoE) is unconfirmed, and there is no evidence of advocacy (no introduction to the VP, no internal docs shared, no competitive wedge internalized). The champion strength is weak: single-threaded, cooling engagement, no VP access demonstrated, and no active selling on our behalf. The risk is high: if Guillaume deprioritizes or lacks internal influence, the deal dies. The move is to re-engage Guillaume, assess his internal standing and access to Cedric Merlet, and if Guillaume cannot or will not facilitate a VP introduction, escalate through Pierre's manager (the deal owner's manager) or seek a partner/SI route to the Economic Buyer.

sources: Salesforce Competitor__c: SAP Ariba
NextStep historical: 'Share the Ariba migration POV + procurement transformation white paper'
Industry context: Ipsen is pharma, Ariba is the dominant pharma procurement platform
competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the named competitor (Salesforce Competitor__c field). Given Ipsen is a large pharma and Ariba is the dominant incumbent in pharma procurement, Ariba is likely the default or incumbent, and the 'make-or-buy discussion' may be a euphemism for 'extend Ariba vs switch to the relevant stakeholder a delivered, internalized competitive wedge (the Ariba migration POV white paper was planned to be shared per NextStep but delivery is unconfirmed), we risk being the RFP also-ran: Ipsen runs a dutiful vendor comparison, Ariba wins on incumbency and integration inertia, and we lose. The how-we-win is: (1) deliver the Ariba migration POV and quantify the TCO advantage (Zycus cost + migration cost vs Ariba renewal + missed AI opportunity cost), (2) make Merlin the centerpiece (Ariba has no agentic AI negotiation capability), and (3) anchor the Merlin story to a pharma-specific use case (supplier price benchmarking for API sourcing, contract compliance for clinical trials). The move is to confirm the Ariba migration POV was received and internalized by Guillaume, and if not, re-send it with a pharma-specific ROI model and position it as the basis for the discovery call.

sources: NextStep historical: 'make-or-buy discussion from the FOP event'
Salesforce Customer_Business_Problem__c, Pain_points_in_the_current_solution__c, What_if_this_is_not_done__c (if present): to be read in Q3 query
identify_pain.narrative: The specific operational or financial pain Ipsen is solving for has not been captured. The NextStep reference to 'make-or-buy discussion from the FOP event' implies Ipsen is evaluating whether to build internal tooling, extend an incumbent, or buy a new platform, which suggests dissatisfaction with the current state, but the pain itself (manual contract redlining, poor spend visibility, slow sourcing cycles, lack of AI-driven negotiation support, compliance risk) is not documented. Without a named pain owner and a quantified impact, the deal lacks urgency and the business case is soft. The move is to use the discovery call to ask: what is broken today, what is the cost of the status quo (time, money, risk), and who in the organization feels that pain most acutely (the pain owner, likely the VP or a senior sourcing leader).

sources: NextStep: no mention of legal, InfoSec, or contracting
Salesforce Current_Contract_Expiration__c, Mandate__c (if present): to be read in Q3 query
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics (MSA, SOW, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec, ERP/integration scope) are unknown. No discussion of contract terms, redlining process, or implementation scoping has occurred. Given Ipsen is a pharma company, InfoSec and data-privacy review (GDPR, supplier data handling) will be mandatory gates, and ERP integration (likely SAP or Oracle) will be a technical validation milestone. The deal is too early (Qualified, pre-RFP) for formal contracting, but the absence of any signal about procurement's internal legal/InfoSec process or the need for an SI partner is a blind spot. The move is to surface this during the discovery call: ask who from Legal, InfoSec, IT, and Finance will be involved post-selection, and what the typical contract timeline looks like for a platform purchase of this size.

sources: Contact roles: Cedric Merlet (VP Commercial Procurement, Decision Maker), Said Tamazirt (Head of Procurement CoE, Decision Maker), Guillaume Quantin (Global Strategic Sourcing Director, Evaluator)
NextStep: no mention of VP or EB engagement
Salesforce X10a_Sponsor__c, Who_will_approve_budget__c (if present): to be read in Q3 query
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer or executive sponsor has been identified or engaged. Cedric Merlet (VP Commercial Procurement, Decision Maker role) and Said Tamazirt (Head of Procurement CoE, Decision Maker role) are both on the contact roster but have no recorded touch. Guillaume Quantin, the sole engaged contact, holds the title Global Strategic Sourcing Director and is role-coded as Evaluator, not EB. In a $200k enterprise S2P deal, budget authority typically sits at VP or C-level (CFO, CPO). Without VP-level sponsorship or a confirmed budget conversation, the deal is vulnerable: the amount may be unvalidated, prioritization is unclear, and the evaluation could be a bottom-up exploration with no executive air-cover. The immediate move is to secure an introduction from Guillaume to Cedric Merlet (the VP) and to position the discovery call or ROI workshop as a VP-level conversation, not a mid-level technical review.

sources: NextStep 25 June 2026: 'Ipsen was supposed to send RFP before summer break'
NextStep historical: 'RFP is confirmed for July 2026'
Contact roles: two Decision Makers (Cedric Merlet, Said Tamazirt), one Evaluator (Guillaume Quantin)
Salesforce What_is_the_decision_process__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c, Multiple_approvals__c (if present): to be read in Q3 query
decision_process.narrative: The decision process is partially visible but not formalized. The planned sequence per NextStep is: formal discovery call with Guillaume (proposed by Pierre for late May 2026, post-FOP event) → RFP issue (planned July 2026 before summer break) → vendor evaluation and demos → selection. The RFP was the forcing event to move from informal exploration (Qualified stage) to structured evaluation (Formal Evaluation stage), but as of 25 June 2026 the RFP has not been issued and Guillaume is non-responsive. This suggests either internal delays (budget hold, prioritization shift, resource constraints) or that the project is lower priority than represented. The contact-role map shows two Decision Makers (Cedric Merlet VP, Said Tamazirt Head of CoE) and one Evaluator (Guillaume), implying a multi-approval process, but the approval chain and the role of IT, InfoSec, Legal, and Finance are unconfirmed. The move is to re-engage Guillaume and ask explicitly: what changed between the FOP event (where RFP July was confirmed) and now, who else is involved in the go/no-go decision, and what would accelerate RFP issue.

sources: Products__c: iContract;iSource;eProcurement;iRequest;Merlin;iRisk;ANA
NextStep: 'make-or-buy discussion from the FOP event'
Salesforce Business_Requirements__c, Top_Challenges_Priorities__c, AI_Needs_in_RFP_Rating__c (if present): to be read in Q3 query
decision_criteria.narrative: The decision criteria are not documented. The Products field indicates full S2P suite scope (iContract, iSource, eProcurement, iRequest, Merlin, iRisk, ANA), suggesting a comprehensive platform evaluation, but the specific evaluation weights (e.g. AI/Merlin capability vs core S2P vs integration vs price vs references) are unknown. The NextStep mention of 'make-or-buy discussion' implies Ipsen may be weighing a build option or an incumbent (Ariba) extension against a new platform, but the trade-off criteria (TCO, time-to-value, change-management risk, AI readiness) have not been surfaced. Without validated criteria, we risk misaligning our demo and proposal to what Ipsen actually cares about. The RFP (planned July 2026) should carry the criteria, but it has not arrived. The move is to use the discovery call to extract the must-haves, nice-to-haves, and knock-outs, and to pre-position Merlin as the AI differentiation criterion before the RFP locks the evaluation framework.

items.detail: RFP planned July 2026 before summer break has not arrived as of 25 June 2026. NextStep notes Guillaume non-responsive since 15 June 2026; reminder planned early July after 6 July 2026. If RFP slips to post-summer (September 2026 or later), evaluation and contracting timeline compresses, risking close-date slip or Q1 2027 push. Summer-holiday timing (June-August 2026 in Europe) may stall buyer engagement for 8-10 weeks.

No Economic Buyer or executive sponsor identified or engaged. Cedric Merlet (VP Commercial Procurement, Decision Maker role) and Said Tamazirt (Head of CoE, Decision Maker role) have no documented contact. Without VP-level sponsorship, budget approval and project prioritization are unconfirmed, and the deal risks being a bottom-up evaluation with no executive air-cover.

Single-threaded on Guillaume Quantin (Evaluator), who has gone silent since 15 June 2026. If Guillaume deprioritizes, leaves, or lacks internal influence, we have no other buyer relationships and the deal dies. No multi-threading to Decision Makers (Cedric Merlet, Said Tamazirt) or to IT/InfoSec.

No pricing, commercials, or budget discussion has occurred. Amount (200k) may be a placeholder or a rough estimate, but it is not validated by the buyer. Without an EB conversation or an RFP with budget range, pricing risk (we underprice and leave money on table, or we overprice and are eliminated early) is high.

Ariba competition risk. SAP Ariba is likely the incumbent or default choice. Without a delivered, internalized Merlin AI wedge and a quantified ROI model showing Zycus TCO advantage, we risk being RFP-response also-rans. The Ariba migration POV (planned share-out per NextStep 15 June 2026) has not been confirmed delivered.

north_star_verdict.math: Days to close: 183 (close 2026-12-31). Age: 65 days since created 2026-04-27. Time in Qualified stage: at least 65 days (no submission-date series available to compute true stage age). LastActivityDate 2026-06-15 is 16 days ago, within the 14-day fresh window, but that activity is rep-side outreach (NextStep update 25 June 2026 notes 'No feedback from Guillaume' and plans a reminder early July after 6 July 2026), not a buyer touch. Last verified buyer engagement is unknown from Salesforce; Avoma discovery will clarify. Forward slip: none recorded in field history. Required pace: for a typical 12-15 month S2P cycle, 183 days is roughly 6 months, placing the deal at mid-cycle; Qualified stage should progress to Formal Evaluation or Shortlisted within 60-90 days of RFP issue. If RFP slips to post-summer (September 2026), the evaluation timeline compresses and close-date defense weakens.
evidence: NextStep 25 June 2026: 'No feedback from Guillaume - Reminder to be sent Early July as Ipsen was supposed to send RFP before summer break. To Do: Call and Chase Guillaume after 6th of July.' Confidence stated as 10%.
NextStep 15 June 2026: 'Ask for project confirmation propose a demonstration meeting on site.' Confidence 10%.

NextStep historical: 'Pierre has proposed a formal discovery call with Guillaume before the end of May, post-meeting in the FOP event — the RFP is confirmed for July 2026. Follow up on the make-or-buy discussion from the FOP event. Share the Ariba migration POV + procurement transformation white paper.'

Contact roles: Guillaume Quantin (Evaluator), Cedric Merlet (Decision Maker, VP Commercial Procurement), Said Tamazirt (Decision Maker, Head of Procurement CoE). No documented engagement with the two Decision Makers.
Products: full S2P suite plus Merlin (iContract, iSource, eProcurement, iRequest, Merlin, iRisk, ANA).
north_star_verdict.headline: RFP slippage risk with summer holiday timing and single-threaded engagement on a silent evaluator. Guillaume Quantin (the sole engaged contact) has not responded since 15 June 2026; planned RFP issue (July 2026 before summer break) has not materialized as of 25 June 2026. No Economic Buyer or VP-level engagement. 183 days to close on a 12-15 month enterprise motion leaves time, but one contributor (no EB, single-threaded on a cooling contact) plus the RFP delay combine to elevate risk.

flags: Position Merlin AI as the wedge against Ariba before the RFP drops. The NextStep instructs sharing the Ariba migration POV and procurement transformation white paper, but there is no evidence this was delivered or that Guillaume engaged with it. Ariba is likely the incumbent or default choice; without a clear AI-driven differentiation story delivered and internalized by the buyer before the RFP, we risk being RFP-response also-rans. The next engagement (early July 2026) must lead with Merlin: show a live negotiation-automation or contract-intelligence use case on a pharma manufacturing-procurement scenario (supplier price benchmarking, contract redlining, savings identification) that Ariba cannot match. Make Merlin the centerpiece of the discovery-call agenda and the demo, and anchor it to a quantified outcome (e.g., 15 percent negotiation time saved, 200k spend captured) so it becomes a must-have criterion in the RFP.

Diagnose the RFP delay and secure a committed restart date. The RFP was confirmed for July 2026 before summer break (per NextStep 15 June 2026), but as of 25 June 2026 it has not arrived and Guillaume is non-responsive. This could signal internal budget delays, competing priorities, executive skepticism about the make-or-buy decision, or simple summer-holiday timing. When Pierre reaches out after 6 July 2026, the conversation must open with: 'What's changed on your end? Is the RFP still on track, or has the timeline shifted? What do you need from us to keep momentum?' If the project is on hold, ask for a frank assessment of priority and executive air-cover; if it is merely delayed, lock a new RFP date and a discovery-call date in writing (calendar invite) so the deal does not drift into Q4 2026 with no progress.

Pierre has proposed a formal discovery call with Guillaume before the end of May, post-meeting in the FOP event — the RFP is confirmed for July 2026. Follow up on the make-or-buy discussion from the FOP event. Share the Ariba migration POV + procurement transformation white paper.

When Pierre re-engages after 6 July 2026, the first ask must be an introduction to Cedric Merlet (VP, budget holder) to validate executive sponsorship and to Said Tamazirt (CoE Head) to understand procurement governance and RFP process. A second thread to IT or InfoSec (to surface integration and data-privacy requirements early) would further de-risk single-threading and create multiple paths into the account.

deltas.subject: Ariba migration POV white paper delivery unconfirmed; competitive wedge at risk

Multi-thread immediately to de-risk Guillaume's silence. Guillaume Quantin has gone dark since 15 June 2026 (11 days ago), and we have zero contact with the two Decision Makers (Cedric Merlet, VP; Said Tamazirt, CoE Head). If Guillaume deprioritizes or leaves, the deal dies. When Pierre re-engages after 6 July 2026, the first ask must be an introduction to Cedric Merlet (VP, budget holder) to validate executive sponsorship and to Said Tamazirt (CoE Head) to understand procurement governance and RFP process. A second thread to IT or InfoSec (to surface integration and data-privacy requirements early) would further de-risk single-threading and create multiple paths into the account.

Position Merlin AI as the wedge against Ariba before the RFP drops. The NextStep instructs sharing the Ariba migration POV and procurement transformation white paper, but there is no evidence this was delivered or that Guillaume engaged with it. Ariba is likely the incumbent or default choice; without a clear AI-driven differentiation story delivered and internalized by the buyer before the RFP, we risk being RFP-response also-rans. The next engagement (early July 2026) must lead with Merlin: show a live negotiation-automation or contract-intelligence use case on a pharma manufacturing-procurement scenario (supplier price benchmarking, contract redlining, savings identification) that Ariba cannot match. Make Merlin the centerpiece of the discovery-call agenda and the demo, and anchor it to a quantified outcome (e.g., 15 percent negotiation time saved, 200k spend captured) so it becomes a must-have criterion in the RFP.

Diagnose the RFP delay and secure a committed restart date. The RFP was confirmed for July 2026 before summer break (per NextStep 15 June 2026), but as of 25 June 2026 it has not arrived and Guillaume is non-responsive. This could signal internal budget delays, competing priorities, executive skepticism about the make-or-buy decision, or simple summer-holiday timing. When Pierre reaches out after 6 July 2026, the conversation must open with: 'What's changed on your end? Is the RFP still on track, or has the timeline shifted? What do you need from us to keep momentum?' If the project is on hold, ask for a frank assessment of priority and executive air-cover; if it is merely delayed, lock a new RFP date and a discovery-call date in writing (calendar invite) so the deal does not drift into Q4 2026 with no progress.
